serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of thyme is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2g) of dried herb, used as a seasoning in soups, stews, marinades, and roasted dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Thyme, fresh — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 170931
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for thyme, fresh (FDC ID 170931) used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore source values for energy (283 kcal), protein (9.1g), fat (7.4g), carbohydrate (26.9g), sugar (1.7g), sodium (55mg), dietary fibre (37g), potassium (814mg), calcium (1890mg), iron (123.6mg) and cholesterol (0mg) are preserved from the input record as they originate from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, an approved primary source. USDA FoodData Central corroborates the macro profile for dried thyme (energy ~276 kcal, protein ~9.1g, fat ~7.4g, carbohydrate ~24.5g per 100g dried). The HPB values are retained as the primary source per source priority rules. Note: iron value of 123.6mg/100g in the HPB record is exceptionally high compared to USDA (17.5mg/100g for fresh thyme); this discrepancy is flagged in notes. Saturated fat sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170931): 2.1g/100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Herbs and Spices — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate herb category
gi_source.source_match_notes: Thyme does not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. As a dried herb with very low available carbohydrate content and high dietary fibre, thyme is assigned an estimated GI of approximately 5 (low), consistent with the GI category applied to other dried culinary herbs and spices with negligible digestible carbohydrate loads. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 0.538) / 100 ≈ 0.03, where serving = 2g and carbohydrate per 2g = 0.538g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (55mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (814mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Thyme is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content and high potassium level strongly support DASH dietary principles, and typical culinary use of 1–5g per dish contributes negligible sodium while providing beneficial potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (37g/100g), very low sugar (1.7g/100g), and polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, thymol) with evidence of improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thyme is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its high fibre content, negligible sugar, and low glycemic load (GL ≈ 0.03 per teaspoon serving) make it a safe and potentially beneficial culinary herb, with polyphenolic compounds showing evidence of supporting insulin sensitivity in preclinical studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (2.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (thymol, carvacrol, rosmarinic acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thyme is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible cholesterol, low saturated fat, high potassium, and rich polyphenol content (thymol, carvacrol, rosmarinic acid) contribute anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective effects when used regularly as a culinary herb.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, thymol, carvacrol) and high dietary fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thyme is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its well-documented anti-inflammatory polyphenols qualify it under the anti-inflammatory herbs criterion, and culinary use is safe, though medicinal-dose thyme supplements should be discussed with a rheumatologist if the patient is on methotrexate or biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thyme (Thymus vulgaris) is a small-leafed aromatic herb in the mint family (Lamiaceae), native to the Mediterranean region and widely used in Western, Middle Eastern, and Caribbean cuisines. The herb features tiny green leaves on woody stems with a distinctive earthy, minty, and slightly floral flavour profile. Thyme is used both fresh and dried as a culinary seasoning in soups, stews, roasted meats, marinades, sauces, and herb blends such as bouquet garni and herbes de Provence. Nutritionally, dried thyme is a concentrated source of dietary fibre (37g/100g), calcium (1890mg/100g), and potassium (814mg/100g), with moderate protein (9.1g/100g) and fat (7.4g/100g). Its energy density is 283 kcal/100g, though typical culinary use is only 1–5g per dish, making the absolute macronutrient contribution per serving very small. Thyme contains significant polyphenolic compounds including thymol, carvacrol, and rosmarinic acid, which have well-documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. These bioactive compounds have been studied for potential benefits in insulin sensitivity, cardiovascular protection, and immune modulation. Thyme is low in sodium (55mg/100g) and contains zero cholesterol, making it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Its very low glycemic load per serving (approximately 0.03) makes it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Thyme is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is a staple seasoning across many global cuisines.
common_user_queries: Can I use thyme if I have high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of thyme?
How much thyme should I use in cooking?
Is dried thyme the same as fresh thyme nutritionally?
Is thyme suitable for a low-sodium diet?
What is the glycemic index of thyme?
meal_context: herb for roasted meats and vegetables

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (an approved primary source) with 0.95 confidence, corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for thyme. Saturated fat and micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170931). GI is an analogous estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate herbs. One data anomaly is noted: the HPB iron value (123.6mg/100g) is substantially higher than USDA values (17.5mg/100g for fresh thyme) and is flagged for review.
notes: 1. IRON ANOMALY: The input HPB record reports iron at 123.6mg/100g, which is exceptionally high compared to USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170931) which reports 17.45mg/100g for fresh thyme and approximately 123.6mg/100g for dried thyme per some older USDA SR Legacy entries. Cross-checking USDA SR Legacy (NDB 02042, Spices, thyme, dried) confirms iron at 123.6mg/100g for dried thyme — this value is therefore retained as it is consistent with the dried form per USDA SR Legacy and HPB. 2. DIETARY TAG CORRECTION: The input record included 'wholegrain' as a dietary tag for thyme, which is incorrect; thyme is an herb, not a grain. This tag has been removed. Correct tags are: low_sodium, high_fibre, vegan, vegetarian, gluten_free. 3. SATURATED FAT: Saturated fat (2.1g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.5g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.1g/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170931 (thyme, fresh); values for dried thyme are proportionally higher but USDA SR Legacy dried thyme (NDB 02042) reports saturated fat at 2.1g/100g, consistent with the HPB energy/macro profile. 4. VITAMIN C: Vitamin C at 160mg/100g is consistent with USDA SR Legacy for dried thyme (NDB 02042: 160.1mg/100g). 5. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 — confirmed zero for this natural herb per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0 as thyme is a plant-based herb containing no vitamin B12 or vitamin D (naturally zero for plant foods). 6. GI: No direct GI measurement for thyme exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 5 is an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate dried herb. 7. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Thyme is a Mediterranean herb used globally; Chinese (百里香), Malay (Timi), Tamil (தைம்), and Hindi (थाइम) names are provided as this herb is used in Singapore and the broader Asian context. No mandatory Singaporean dish multilingual requirement applies as thyme is not a Singaporean dish; names are provided for completeness and retrieval coverage.
